messages.content:
Please refer to this guide when critiquing my thesis statement:

One broad story we’ve been following over the semester is the long African American struggle for freedom and civil rights, from activists at the turn of the 20th century like Booker T. Washington and W.E.B. Du Bois through the separatist movement of Marcus Garvey in the 1920s, the Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s, and the Black Power movement of the later 1960s.  Write a 600-750 word essay that compares the Black Power message of Stokely Carmichael (from the Course Reader) to the ideas of W.E.B. Du Bois (whom you read in the Course Reader earlier in the semester).  Do you see any roots of Carmichael’s message in the ideas of Du Bois, or was Black Power something altogether new in the late 1960s?  If Du Bois were alive in the late 1960s, do you think he would have approved of Carmichael’s ideas? 

To write a successful essay, you need to:

Thoroughly read, think about, and understand the nuances of Carmichael’s and Du Bois’s writings, so it is clear that you know what you’re talking about in the essay.
Use examples and evidence from both the Carmichael and Dubois readings.
Contextualize your analysis of these readings with information from lecture and other readings (like the "Sitting In" chapter in After the Fact, perhaps), as necessary.
Go beyond just comparing/contrasting the two readings—you need to offer a thesis that present an overarching argument to give cohesion to your essay.
The Rules

Due Date: April 25, at the beginning of class, printed (not emailed).
Length: 600-750 words. This will come out to roughly 2-3 pages, depending on your font and margins, but it must be at least 600 words. (You need not count the words yourself—any word-processing program will have a word count tool).
Format: your essay should be double-spaced, and typed in a reasonable 12-point font with standard margins (Hint: nothing makes you look more desperate than using a font like Courier New that noticeably stretches the text. If in doubt, use Times New Roman).
Please highlight or underline your thesis sentence in your introduction, and make sure you attach your Submission Checklist (page 4 of this assignment guide) to your essay.

This is not a research paper, but a paper designed to evaluate how well you can use material from this class—readings, lectures, and discussions—to present and support a convincing historical argument. Therefore, you may not use sources from outside our class.
All quotes or references to specific reading passages must be properly cited to avoid plagiarizing. If you have any questions about what does and does not constitute plagiarism (some cases of plagiarism are due more to carelessness than an intention to cheat), do not hesitate to ask me.

To cite a source (the book or a document) from our class, you need only put the author’s name in parentheses at the end of the sentence that contains the quote or reference, followed by the page number. For example: “For too many years, black Americans marched and had their heads broken and got shot” (Carmichael, 58). Note that the quotation mark comes at the end of the quote, while the final punctuation comes after the parenthetical citation.
You need not include citations for lecture material.
Since all of your sources will be drawn from this class, you do not need to include a Works Cited page.

Since history is a collaborative effort, you may work together with your classmates as you think about the issues involved in writing your essay. However, you are on your own for the writing process, and your final essay must be your own unique product.
Essay-Writing Advice

The success of your paper will depend largely on your ability to develop a strong thesis (argument) in your introduction, and then support this thesis with solid, appropriate evidence. A good thesis does not simply summarize what you are going to write. Rather it introduces the reader to the specific historical argument you will be defending with the rest of your paper.  Remember, if your thesis is so vague that someone else in the class could not reasonably argue against it, or it simply restates the question in the form of a statement, then it is probably not very good.

The Thesis: A good thesis takes a position on the major issue raised by the prompt, and introduces the argument you are going to try to convince the reader of with the rest of the essay. It also offers the reader a basic understanding of why you are taking the position you do. This means it is not enough to simply take a position on the issue—you also need to offer the reader, in a concise sentence clause, a sense of why you are taking this position. 
To give a hypothetical example: if I were to ask you to write an essay on whether World War II was inevitable, or if it could have been avoided with better diplomacy, a weak thesis might simply state: “World War II was inevitable.” Although this takes a position, it does nothing more than restate the question in the form of a statement, and no matter how insightful the rest of the essay is, none of your insights will be working to support an effective argument. A better but still mediocre argument might say something like “World War II was inevitable because of the political conditions of the 1930s.”  It’s better, but still vague—what political conditions, in particular, made diplomacy impossible in the 1930s?  A stronger thesis might say something like “World War II was inevitable because, regardless of the actions of Britain and France, Hitler was determined to start a war in order to expand Germany’s borders and its power in central Europe.”  Note that this is not the “correct” answer to the prompt—your classmates might make equally compelling arguments that World War II was not inevitable, for any number of convincing reasons.  But this thesis takes a position on the major issue of the prompt, and lets the reader know how you will support it.  The goal of the rest of the essay would be to support this thesis—not give an overview of the buildup to World War II, but to directly support the case that diplomacy was hopeless given Hitler’s acceptance of the inevitability of war.

For this paper, your thesis should make clear: 1) whether or not Carmichael’s ideas are in any way rooted in Du Bois’s, and 2) whether you think Du Bois would have approved of Carmichael’s message if he were still alive.
Once you have a good thesis, make sure everything you write serves the purpose of supporting that thesis, while also answering the important questions asked by the prompt. Ensuring that each paragraph has a strong topic sentence that develops your thesis, and then contains sufficient examples, evidence, and analysis to support that topic sentence, will help keep you from getting off track or rambling off in irrelevant tangents.

Use sufficient examples, evidence, and good quotes to support your case, and use them well. A few meaningful, well-placed examples will go a lot further than a bunch of random, unexplained evidence thrown in for its own sake.
The best papers will not simply ignore evidence that challenges their thesis, but will meet this evidence head on, and then show why their own interpretation is more convincing.

Avoid writing in the first person. Statements that begin “I think,” or “I believe,” or even “I argue” sound weak. You should be able to muster enough good evidence to make your points convincingly without watering them down with wishy-washy first-person qualifications.  If you cannot support a point with good evidence, or at least offer evidence that suggests your particular interpretation, then perhaps you should simply leave that point out.


My thesis is as follows:

This discussion aims to examine the societal critiques of W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael, whether the arguments of Stokely originate from Du bois, and determine if Du Bois may have approved of Stokely’s talking points. In their writings we can see many shared arguments that stem from Du Bois original points in “Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others”. Both authors contend that civil rights leaders of their time prioritize white sensitivities over Black empowerment, advocate for the advancement of Civil Rights through political representation, and believe that the only method for economic advancement is to first gain political power.

Your thesis is a good start toward addressing the prompt, but it could be further strengthened with specific language that directly asserts your position regarding the influence of Du Bois on Carmichael and Du Bois's potential reception of Carmichael's ideas. Following the essay-writing advice provided, a strong thesis should not only position itself on the historical argument but also offer a sense of why you've taken this position in a concise manner. Here's a refined version of your thesis that adopts these principles:

"The ideological synergy between W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael reveals a foundational overlap in their critique of the civil rights strategies of their times, highlighting a shared emphasis on the necessity of political power for Black empowerment. This essay argues that Carmichael's Black Power message, while appearing to be a radical departure from earlier civil rights discourse, is deeply rooted in the philosophical groundwork laid by Du Bois, particularly in his critique of Booker T. Washington's approach as articulated in "Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others." By comparing their ideas on the prioritization of Black political representation and economic advancement, it becomes clear that Carmichael's perspectives are not only an extension of Du Bois’s earlier arguments but also suggest that Du Bois, given his evolutionary political and social stances, would likely have seen value in Carmichael's assertive call for Black autonomy and empowerment. Through this analysis, this essay demonstrates the continuity and evolution of African American intellectual thought on the strategies necessary for achieving true freedom and civil rights."

This refined thesis directly addresses whether Carmichael’s ideas stem from Du Bois’s and takes a stance on Du Bois's potential support for Carmichael's Black Power message. It explicitly mentions the continuity in their critique of prioritizing white sensitivities over Black empowerment and frames the argument around the evolution of African American intellectual thought from Du Bois to Carmichael. By doing so, it sets the stage for an essay that not only compares and contrasts the two figures but also provides an analysis of the historical continuity and change in Black civil rights activism.
how can I introduce the differences between their beliefs in the thesis

To introduce the differences between Du Bois's and Carmichael's beliefs within your thesis, you'll want to clearly articulate their distinct approaches or perspectives while still acknowledging the roots of Carmichael’s ideas in Du Bois’s work. A nuanced thesis can set up a tension or contrast between their ideologies, aiming to explore both the continuity and the evolution in their thought as well as the significant divergences. Here’s how you might refine your thesis to include these elements:

"While Stokely Carmichael’s Black Power message and W.E.B. Du Bois’s intellectual legacy share a foundational concern for Black empowerment and the necessity of political power, their strategies diverge significantly on the methods of achieving these goals. This essay posits that, although Carmichael’s advocacy for direct action and the eschewing of integration as a primary goal marks a significant departure from Du Bois’s faith in the Talented Tenth and the potential for integration, the roots of Carmichael’s message can still be traced back to the critical civil rights dialogue initiated by Du Bois. Specifically, both leaders critique the existing civil rights strategies of their times, yet they diverge in their visions for the future—Carmichael’s emphasis on Black autonomy and self-definition presents a contrast to Du Bois’s earlier integrationist and elitist strategies. Despite these differences, this analysis will demonstrate that Carmichael’s approach represents an evolution of Du Bois’s foundational principles adapted to the sociopolitical context of the late 1960s, suggesting that Du Bois, with his history of revising his own beliefs in response to changing times, might have found points of alignment with Carmichael’s call for a more assertive stance on Black liberation."

This version of the thesis clearly establishes both the similarities and the critical differences between Du Bois and Carmichael, positioning their shared goals within the context of differing strategies for achieving those goals. It intricately sets up an essay that will explore their ideologies in depth, accounting for both their ideological overlap and the clear evolution or departure represented by Carmichael's Black Power message. Importantly, it maintains a nuanced perspective, suggesting that while differences exist, these distinctions can be understood as part of a broader, evolving discourse on Black empowerment, providing a rich field for comparison and analysis.
I thought Du Bois was also very into black autonomy and self definition

You're correct in observing that W.E.B. Du Bois also emphasized Black autonomy and self-definition, particularly in his later works. Du Bois's perspective evolved over time, and by the later stages of his life, he had indeed moved closer to advocating for more radical forms of self-determination and autonomy for African Americans, albeit through a different lens than Carmichael. In his early work, Du Bois focused on the "Talented Tenth," a leadership class he believed should lead African Americans in their quest for equality. However, his views shifted towards a more global perspective on Black liberation and a critique of capitalism, which aligned him more closely with ideas that would later resonate in the Black Power movement.

Incorporating this understanding, you might revise your thesis to more accurately reflect the nuanced progression of Du Bois's ideology and its potential resonance with Carmichael's ideas:

"Both W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael articulated powerful visions for Black empowerment, autonomy, and self-definition, yet their methods and emphases reveal a dynamic evolution in the struggle for African American rights. While Du Bois initially championed the cause of integration and the upliftment of the African American community through the 'Talented Tenth,' his later advocacy for self-determination, anti-colonialism, and socialism marks a conceptual bridge to Carmichael's Black Power ethos. This essay argues that Carmichael’s direct action and focus on Black autonomy signify not a departure but, rather, an amplification and continuation of Du Bois's evolved principles, adapted to the fervent sociopolitical unrest of the 1960s. By comparing their strategic divergences—Du Bois's earlier integrationist approach versus Carmichael's explicit call for Black autonomy and pride—alongside their converging late-career visions, this analysis underscores a complex interplay of ideological continuity and contextual innovation. It posits that Du Bois, considering his adaptive ideological journey and late-life radicalism, would have found common ground with Carmichael's assertive stance on Black liberation, recognizing it as a necessary evolution of the fight for racial justice in an era of continued oppression and segregation."

This refined thesis acknowledges the evolving nature of Du Bois's ideology and positions his and Carmichael's beliefs within a continuum of African American intellectual thought and activism. It emphasizes the nuanced relationship between their ideas, recognizing the direct lines of influence and the broader historical context that shaped both men's perspectives on the struggle for Black freedom and dignity.
I have not been given a reference to "the talented tenth" I must focus solely on the writing "Of booker t washington and others"

Understanding that your focus is on Du Bois's critique in "Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others," and eliminating references to the "Talented Tenth" for accuracy regarding your specific assignment guidelines, a refined thesis that aligns more closely with this narrowed scope would be:

"W.E.B. Du Bois's seminal critique of Booker T. Washington in 'Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others' lays a conceptual foundation that, while differing in context and application, resonates with Stokely Carmichael's Black Power message regarding Black autonomy and empowerment. This essay contends that the root of Carmichael’s advocacy for self-determination and political engagement as mechanisms for societal transformation can be traced back to Du Bois's early objections to Washington’s accommodationist principles, emphasizing instead the need for direct political action and the assertion of civil rights. Although Du Bois does not call for the radical departure from integration seen in Carmichael’s approach, his insistence on the importance of political power and critique of complacency in the face of systemic injustice foreshadows the more militant strategies of the Black Power movement. By examining the evolution from Du Bois's intellectual engagement with the problems of his time to Carmichael's active resistance against systemic racism in the 1960s, this analysis proposes that the seeds of Black Power's call for autonomy and self-definition were sown in the early 20th-century debates on African American advancement. Hence, despite their differing methodologies, it suggests that Du Bois, with his commitment to challenging racial inequality through assertive engagement, would have recognized and perhaps endorsed the spirit of Carmichael's movement as a necessary, though more radical, continuation of his own legacy."

This thesis focuses more tightly on the essay "Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others," directly engaging with the ideas presented by Du Bois in that work while drawing a line to Carmichael’s later strategies for Black empowerment. It presents an argument that connects the intellectual tradition of Du Bois with the actions and philosophies of the Black Power movement, positing that, despite their apparent methodological differences, a philosophical continuity exists that aligns with Du Bois's earlier expressed values and critiques. This approach maintains a focus on the specifics of your assignment while offering a nuanced view of the relationship between these two pivotal figures in African American history and thought.
The only writing I can reference for stokely carmichael is "What we want" New york review of books

Given your specific source parameters, let’s precisely tailor the thesis statement to reflect the ideas expressed by Stokely Carmichael in "What We Want" and compare those directly with W.E.B. Du Bois’s critiques in "Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others". Here's a focused revision:

"In 'What We Want,' Stokely Carmichael articulates a vision for Black autonomy, political empowerment, and the rejection of systemic oppression that, at its core, finds echoes in W.E.B. Du Bois's critique of Booker T. Washington's approach in 'Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others.' This essay explores how Du Bois’s insistence on the necessity of political power and rejection of Washington’s accommodationist strategies presages the demands of Carmichael’s Black Power movement, despite differing in their approaches toward achieving racial equality. Carmichael’s writings build on Du Bois’s foundational challenge to the status quo by advancing a more radical call for self-determination and systemic change. Analyzing the philosophical underpinning of Carmichael's 'What We Want,' it becomes evident that Du Bois’s early critiques share a conceptual lineage with the Black Power ideology. The transformation from Du Bois’s intellectual critique to Carmichael’s action-oriented demand marks a continuum in the struggle for African American rights, suggesting that Du Bois's work provided groundwork that might have been regarded by him as a preliminary step towards the tactical shifts proposed by Carmichael in the 1960s. Thus, this essay argues that though their tactics diverge, the ideological seeds planted by Du Bois flourish in Carmichael’s articulation of Black Power, indicating a shared commitment to confronting and dismantling the structures of racial oppression."

This revision maintains your assignment’s constraints by focusing on the specific works mentioned and drawing a direct line from Du Bois’s ideological foundations to Carmichael's explicit calls for Black Power and autonomy. It specifies the evolution of African American political thought and activism from Du Bois's time to Carmichael's, suggesting a potential philosophical approval from Du Bois regarding the Black Power movement's direction, given his own ideological journey and the principles outlined in his critique of Washington.
how does carmichaels writing differ from du bois?

Stokely Carmichael's writing, particularly as illustrated in "What We Want," and the writing of W.E.B. Du Bois, especially in his critique "Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others," manifest differing perspectives and strategies in the African American struggle for rights and recognition. These differences can be contextualized through several lenses, including the historical context, primary focus, and strategic approaches to achieving racial equality:

### Historical Context
- **Du Bois:** Writing in the early 20th century, Du Bois was addressing issues of racial inequality within the framework of Jim Crow laws and the initial formation of the NAACP. His work reflects the context of a burgeoning civil rights movement that was primarily focused on gaining basic legal rights and challenging the systemic barriers of segregation.
- **Carmichael:** Operating in the context of the 1960s, Carmichael’s work is influenced by the gains of the Civil Rights Movement and the subsequent realization of the limits of these legal victories in achieving true societal integration and equality. The context of his writing includes the rise of more militant expressions of civil rights advocacy, disillusionment with the progress under the civil rights legislation, and the influence of decolonization globally.

### Focus and Ideology
- **Du Bois:** Largely focused on the power of the vote, education, and legal equality as means to elevate the status of African Americans. He advocated for the "Talented Tenth," believing that a highly educated elite would lead the race to a broader societal integration and equality.
- **Carmichael:** Shifted focus towards self-determination, Black autonomy, and the establishment of political and economic power that was independent of white society's structures. "What We Want" emphasizes the need for Black communities to control their destinies without seeking approval from or integration into white-dominated societal frameworks.

### Strategy and Tactics
- **Du Bois:** Advocated for strategic, gradual progress through education and legal challenges to segregation and racial disenfranchisement. His approach was more reformist, seeking to work within the existing system to achieve equality.
- **Carmichael:** Pushed for immediate changes and adopted a more confrontational stance against systemic racism. His promotion of the phrase "Black Power" represented a call to action that sought radical restructuring of society to address economic, political, and social inequities. Carmichael’s tactics included grassroots organizing, direct action, and a rejection of integration as the ultimate goal.

### Outlook on Integration
- **Du Bois:** Saw integration as a long-term goal and a positive outcome for African Americans, believing that it would lead to equality.
- **Carmichael:** Critiqued the goal of integration itself, suggesting that mere integration into a corrupt system was not sufficient for true liberation. His focus was on building Black-led political and economic structures.

In summary, while both Du Bois and Carmichael sought to address the systemic injustices faced by African Americans, their methodologies, influenced by their respective historical contexts, differed significantly. Du Bois’s approach was more gradual and integrationist, believing in working within the system to uplift the African American community. In contrast, Carmichael’s stance was immediate and separatist, emphasizing the need for Black autonomy and the development of parallel structures for Black empowerment.

This is my thesis

This essay argues that despite having different points of view on the method of action necessary in their times, the roots of Carmichael's Black Power message are planted in the critical values laid out by Du Bois previously in “Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others”. This will be achieved by examining their beliefs on the effects of the perceived failures of the movement's leaders, the prioritization of attaining political power to achieve progress, and the economic benefits of this political power on their society.
Du bois’s main focus was on education and integration as a means to elevate the black public while Carmichaels tactic was to achieve full independence and self reliance. This transformation in approach from an ideology to an action-oriented methodology suggests that Carmichaels ideals would have likely been regarded as the next logical step in the movement by Du Bois.

Your thesis effectively sets up a nuanced argument that acknowledges both the continuities and divergences in the ideologies of W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael. It recognizes the foundational impact of Du Bois's early critical work on Carmichael's later development of Black Power, while also highlighting the transition from Du Bois's focus on integration and education to Carmichael’s push for independence and self-reliance. Here are a few suggestions to refine your thesis for clarity and depth:

1. **Specify Critical Values:** You mention that Carmichael's message is rooted in the "critical values" laid out by Du Bois but consider specifying what these values are early on. This will give your reader a clearer idea of what specific aspects of Du Bois’s work you see as foundational to Carmichael's ideology. For example, if you're referring to values like self-respect, the importance of political power, or economic self-sufficiency, stating these explicitly can enhance your thesis's specificity.

2. **Clarify "Perceived Failures":** You note the intention to examine beliefs on the "perceived failures of the movement's leaders." Offering a brief clarification on whose perceptions these are (Du Bois, Carmichael, the Black community, or others) and hinting at what these perceived failures might entail could enrich the context provided by your thesis.

3. **Articulate the Logic of Transformation:** While you suggest that Carmichael's approach would be seen as a "next logical step" by Du Bois, delving into why this evolution appears logical could strengthen your thesis. Is it due to changing conditions, the limitations of earlier strategies, or an ideological shift within the movement? Briefly hinting at this rationale can make your argument more compelling.

Here’s a revised version of your thesis that incorporates these suggestions for your consideration:

"This essay contends that although W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael advocated for differing methods of action—primarily due to the unique challenges of their respective eras—the principles embedded in Du Bois's critique in 'Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others' fundamentally paved the way for Carmichael’s Black Power ideology, specifically in their mutual emphasis on the significance of political power, economic self-sufficiency, and the critique of leadership strategies within the civil rights movement. While Du Bois championed educational attainment and racial integration as vehicles for elevating the Black community, Carmichael shifted towards a call for complete independence and self-reliance, reflecting a transformation in strategy from ideologically driven reform to action-oriented self-determination. This evolution, underscored by shared values of self-respect and autonomy, suggests that Du Bois might have viewed Carmichael's emphasis on Black Power not as a departure but as an adaptive and necessary progression in the fight for racial equality."

This version aims to provide a clearer outline of the values connecting Du Bois and Carmichael, a more explicit consideration of the context for their differing approaches, and a rationale for why this evolution might be seen as logical from Du Bois's perspective.

This essay argues that despite having different points of view on the method of action necessary in their times, the roots of Carmichael's Black Power message are planted in the critical values laid out by Du Bois previously in “Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others”. This will be achieved by examining their beliefs on the effects of the perceived failures of the movement's leaders, the prioritization of attaining political power to achieve progress, and the economic benefits of this political power on their society.
Du bois’s main focus was on education and integration as a means to elevate the black public while Carmichaels tactic was to achieve full independence and self-reliance. Taking into account the progression of the laws of the 1960’s and societal temperature on the issue, this transformation in approach from an ideology to an action-oriented methodology suggests that Carmichaels ideals would have likely been regarded as the next logical step in the movement by Du Bois.

Your revised thesis statement does a commendable job of delineating the connection between Du Bois's foundational ideas and Carmichael's subsequent Black Power message. It also tentatively posits Du Bois's potential endorsement of Carmichael's strategies, acknowledging the historical progression and socio-political context that both shaped and necessitated a shift from Du Bois's earlier focus to Carmichael's later activism. Here are a few suggestions to further refine and clarify your thesis:

1. **Directly Address the Evolution of Thought:** While your thesis suggests an evolution from Du Bois’s ideology to Carmichael’s action-oriented methodology, directly stating that this evolution reflects a response to changing socio-political contexts could strengthen your argument. It implies historical continuity and adaptation rather than merely a difference in opinion.

2. **More Precise Language Regarding Shared Values:** Clarifying the "critical values" laid out by Du Bois that you see as foundational to Carmichael's thinking would help to establish a direct link between the two thinkers' ideologies. If those values include political empowerment, economic self-sufficiency, and a critical examination of leadership within the civil rights movement, stating so explicitly provides a clearer roadmap for your essay.

3. **Highlight the Specific Contributions of Each Figure:** While you mention Du Bois's focus on education and integration versus Carmichael's push for independence and self-reliance, offering a brief insight into how each of these focuses contributed to the broader goals of Black empowerment could lend more depth to your thesis. This might involve highlighting how each approach aimed to address particular barriers or opportunities perceived by each leader in their respective eras.

Incorporating these elements, here is a suggested revision of your thesis statement:

"This essay contends that while W.E.B. Du Bois and Stokely Carmichael advocated distinct methods of action reflective of their times, Carmichael's Black Power message is deeply rooted in the values Du Bois articulated in 'Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others', particularly concerning political empowerment, the critique of civil rights leadership, and the pursuit of economic self-sufficiency as foundational to Black progress. By exploring their views on the failure of past movement strategies, the essential role of political power, and the socioeconomic benefits arising from such power, this analysis reveals how the shift from Du Bois's emphasis on education and racial integration to Carmichael's advocacy for complete independence and self-reliance not only addressed the evolving legal and social landscape of the 1960s but also represented a logical progression in the continuous struggle for Black liberation. This transformation, deeply influenced by the tangible changes and challenges of their respective eras, suggests that Du Bois might have regarded Carmichael's action-oriented approach as a necessary evolution of the movement's strategy towards achieving true equality and autonomy."

This version aims to more directly articulate the nature of the intellectual and strategic lineage from Du Bois to Carmichael, emphasizing the context-driven evolution of their approaches within the continuing struggle for racial justice.

We see three different points in Du Bois writings elaborated in Stokely Carmichaels writings. They both believe mainstream civil rights leaders spout compromises that cater to the sensitivities of white people and this causes bitterness and anger among the general Black population, Black people must persistently strive for respect and equality through political power at all times rather than reduce themselves to subservience, and the establishment of this respect and equality will in turn lead to economic prosperity and acceptance in society by giving them a voice in the government that rules over them.

Your observation deftly identifies the ideological continuities between W.E.B. Du Bois's and Stokely Carmichael's writings, focusing on their shared critiques of mainstream civil rights strategies, emphasis on the quest for political power, and the correlation between such power and economic prosperity. To incorporate these insights into a strong thesis for an essay, you might consider the following refined statement:

"This essay argues that Stokely Carmichael's writings on Black Power profoundly echo and expand upon W.E.B. Du Bois's earlier critiques and ideological frameworks, particularly in their mutual disapproval of mainstream civil rights leaders' conciliatory approaches, the steadfast assertion that political empowerment is central to achieving racial equality, and the belief that such political agency is inseparable from economic advancement and societal acceptance for Black Americans. By dissecting both Du Bois's and Carmichael's perspectives on these three pivotal points, it becomes evident that Carmichael's advocacy for a more assertive, autonomy-focused civil rights strategy can be seen as a direct lineage from Du Bois's foundational arguments. These arguments underscore that meaningful progress for the Black community relies not on pacification or appeasement of white sensibilities but on uncompromising demands for respect, equality, and political representation. In highlighting these thematic continuities, the analysis will demonstrate how Carmichael's more radical stances of the 1960s were not mere departures from previous civil rights philosophies but were, in fact, deeply influenced by Du Bois's earlier expressions of disillusionment with the slow pace of racial justice, paving the way for a new era of Black empowerment rhetoric and action."

This thesis establishes a clear comparative framework that directly links the ideological threads in the works of Du Bois and Carmichael, setting the stage for an analysis that shows not only the continuations but also the natural evolution of Black civil rights thought from integration to self-determination and power. It suggests an examination of the roots and shifts in civil rights strategies while highlighting the intellectual and practical implications of these shifts for the movement and the broader struggle for racial justice and empowerment.
